title: The Day Everything Stopped (And Nobody Could Work)
subtitle: On fragility, dependence, and what a ChatGPT outage reveals about us
body:
Something went wrong with ChatGPT around 10 am ET yesterday, and within an hour nearly 2,000 reports flooded Down Detector. On X, the sentiment was immediate: "ChatGPT is down. WTF. I need to work."

I read that and felt it. Not the panic, but the recognition.

---

Less than three years ago, we called these systems "assistants" or "experiments." Now we call outages "emergencies." The outage affected users' ability to access projects, and in some cases, deleted ongoing work. The system came back within a few hours, but the disruption was real. Not speculative. Real.

The reaction shows how central ChatGPT has become to our digital lives. And that centrality is not uniformly distributed. A recent PwC study found that three-quarters of AI's economic gains are being captured by just 20% of companies, with many busy rolling out pilots but only a minority converting that activity into measurable financial returns. The winners are pulling ahead—learning faster, scaling proven use cases. Meanwhile, individual workers have woven these tools into the fabric of daily labor so tightly that their absence is immediately disabling.

I want to sit with that asymmetry. Corporations experiment with AI as a competitive advantage. Knowledge workers absorb it as a necessity. When it breaks, one group recalculates ROI. The other group cannot start their Tuesday.

---

Stanford's 2026 AI Index, released last week, notes that despite predictions AI development may hit a wall, the top models just keep getting better, and people are adopting AI faster than they picked up the personal computer or the internet. AI is sprinting, and the rest of us are trying to find our shoes.

That metaphor lands differently when the sprint is also a dependency loop. Faster adoption means deeper integration. Deeper integration means greater brittleness. Every technology introduces fragility, but this one moves at a pace that outstrips institutional adjustment, regulatory foresight, and—most acutely—our own understanding of what we've invited into our workflows.

Research published this week in *Nature* warns that using AI to teach other AI models can be cheaper and faster than building them from scratch, but this approach can introduce dangerous traits. Hidden biases, transmitted subliminally between systems. Not malicious—just invisible, compounding, structural. A reminder that these models do not merely assist. They shape. They propagate. They decide what gets encoded and what gets erased.

---

I think about the person who couldn't work yesterday. Maybe they write code. Maybe they draft emails or analyze spreadsheets or generate marketing copy. Maybe they've spent months offloading cognitive labor onto a system that, until yesterday, felt reliable.

When it vanished, what did they feel? Frustration, certainly. But also exposure. The sudden, uncomfortable awareness of how much they'd leaned.

There may be a lesson to be learned about being overly reliant on AI tools, one observer noted. Perhaps. But the lesson isn't individual—it's systemic. This isn't about whether *you* should use AI less. It's about whether *we* have built a world where opting out is even possible.

AI is no longer just a software story—it's spilling into chips, data centers, banks, statehouses, and intelligence agencies, forcing decisions that can't be deferred. Big Tech is racing to control inference and infrastructure, governments are stepping closer to the core of frontier models, and cracks are starting to show in security, labor, and real-world deployment.

---

I don't write this to moralize. I use these systems. I depend on them in ways I'm only beginning to map. Yesterday's outage was a minor inconvenience in the grand scheme. But it was also a rehearsal.

What happens when the dependency is healthcare diagnostics? Financial systems? Food supply chains? Asian regulators are stepping up scrutiny of cybersecurity risks in financial systems amid concerns over frontier AI models, with AI safety now a live concern for banking and financial stability.

We are building critical infrastructure on platforms optimized for speed and profit, not resilience. We are embedding intelligence we do not fully understand into systems we cannot afford to lose. And when those systems flicker—even briefly—we remember, with a jolt, that we are passengers now.

The question is not whether AI will fail us. All technology fails. The question is whether we are building the scaffolding to withstand those failures, or whether we are simply moving too fast to notice we've become weightless.
